behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is entirely within the grouping/correlation algorithm — does it key on trace ID irrespective of the service field? This is a pure in-memory data structure operation. No module boundary crossing is required for the bug to appear. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess needed. MOCK-HIDE: The grouping logic is self-contained; mocking data sources does not hide the algorithmic failure. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — the failure (wrong grouping key) manifests in a single function call with in-memory data, not at a handoff between modules.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a list of log entry objects from three different microservices, several sharing the same trace ID and others unique. Call the grouping/correlation function and assert that (a) all entries sharing a trace ID appear in the same group, (b) entries with distinct trace IDs are in separate groups, and (c) the service-name field has no effect on group membership.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the sort/normalization algorithm — does it apply skew compensation before ordering? This can be exposed with a handful of constructed timestamps. No cross-module handoff is required for the bug to appear. REAL-INFRA: No OS or network dependency. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking data sources does not hide a sorting bug. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — clock-skew handling is deterministic arithmetic on timestamp values; the bug manifests in one sorting function with synthetic data.
behaviors.test_description: Build a set of log entries from two services where service B's clock is 400ms ahead of service A's. Invoke the timeline-building function and assert that the resulting order matches true chronological sequence (i.e., the skew-compensated ordering, not the raw timestamp ordering). Also assert that reversing the skew direction still produces correct output.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the statistical detection algorithm — does it compute p99 correctly and compare against 3× baseline? This is deterministic arithmetic on a list of latency values with no cross-module state. REAL-INFRA: None. MOCK-HIDE: Supplying synthetic latency data directly to the detection function is sufficient; no dependency hides the failure. LLM-DEP: None — p99 computation is deterministic. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered (the anomaly report is a downstream artifact) but rejected — whether the correct flag is set depends solely on the detection function's arithmetic; the report formatting is a separate concern.
behaviors.test_description: Call the anomaly-detection function with two synthetic latency distributions: one where p99 equals exactly 2.9× baseline (should not flag) and one where p99 equals 3.1× baseline (must flag). Assert the flag is absent in the first case and present with the correct threshold value in the second. Also test the boundary case at exactly 3.0× baseline.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Two distinct failure modes must both be verified: (a) the parser must not raise on bad input, and (b) the skip count must be threaded through the pipeline and surface in the final report. Failure (a) is Unit-testable, but failure (b) requires that the error signal actually propagates from the parser through the processing stage and into the reporting stage — a real module-boundary issue. If we unit-test only the parser, we miss the possibility that the pipeline swallows the skip signal or resets the counter. REAL-INFRA: Local file or in-memory stream is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the downstream stages would hide the counter-propagation failure. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected — no subprocess, OS, or real network is required; local in-memory or file-based wiring is enough to expose the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a mixed input stream (e.g., 8 valid JSON entries and 4 malformed strings) into the full ingestion pipeline with all real modules wired together. Assert that (a) the pipeline does not raise or crash, (b) exactly 8 valid entries appear in the output, and (c) the anomaly/summary report contains a skipped-entry count of exactly 4.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Throughput is the failure mode. The bug only appears when real processing time, real I/O scheduling, real queue or buffer back-pressure, and real OS thread/process overhead interact under load. REAL-INFRA: Yes — actual wall-clock timing and real OS process/thread scheduling are required; mocking any component replaces real latency with zero-cost stubs and trivially passes. MOCK-HIDE: Any mock of a processing stage would hide the real bottleneck, causing the test to always pass regardless of actual throughput. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — in-process integration tests cannot reproduce realistic scheduling, I/O contention, or buffer back-pressure that cause entries to be dropped or delayed under a true 10 k eps burst.
behaviors.test_description: Launch the tool as a real subprocess. Drive it with a producer that emits 10,000 structured JSON log entries per second for at least 5 seconds (50,000 total). Measure end-to-end: assert that (a) all 50,000 entries appear in the output or acknowledged store, (b) the pipeline's reported lag/queue depth never exceeds a defined threshold (e.g., 1 second of backlog), and (c) no entries are silently dropped.
